snapshot_html: The regime has cooled to mixed. After five days of strikes, the US and Iran agreed a standdown on Sunday evening and set technical talks in Doha for Tuesday, though Tehran insists the interim deal — including release of roughly $6bn in frozen funds — must be implemented first and still claims Hormuz passage requires Iranian coordination. Markets are leaning into the diplomacy: Brent eased about 1% to the low-$70s (~$72–74) as Gulf transits stayed consistent, unwinding the weekend war premium. Gold slipped to ~$4,030, on track for a fourth straight monthly loss and down over 10% on the month, as a hawkish Fed — held at 3.50–3.75% under new chair Warsh, dot plot flipping to a 2026 hike — trumps the haven bid. The dollar eased to ~101.3 from a 14-month high near 101.8 as traders trimmed hike bets, while USD/JPY stayed pinned near 160. Equities firmed: the S&P 500 rose about 0.4% and the Nikkei added 0.9% after Friday's slide, though the Hang Seng slipped −0.6% to ~22,540. Asia stays a slow-burn second front as Chinese Coast Guard vessels keep near-continuous patrols east of Taiwan and Beijing presses Japan, while Ukraine's summer gains add a de-escalation tilt in Europe. Net: the ceasefire holds but is shaky — respect the talks-led relief, but keep tail hedges cheap until Doha delivers written clarity on Hormuz transit corridors.

scenarios.name: De-escalation / talks hold
scenarios.desc: The Sunday standdown sticks, Doha produces written clarity on Hormuz transit corridors and frozen-asset release, and the interim deal is implemented. Residual war premium keeps bleeding out of oil while risk assets grind higher.
moves: capped by hawkish Fed, haven bid fades

scenarios.name: Frozen conflict / fragile standoff
scenarios.desc: Talks limp on without a clean deal, the standdown holds but Hormuz stays gummed up under Iranian 'coordination', and a sticky war premium lingers in oil and gold. The base case if Doha is inconclusive.

scenarios.name: Re-escalation / wider war
scenarios.desc: Doha collapses, Iran walks from the MOU, and strikes resume with Hormuz throttled or hit as the US is drawn back in. Lower odds after the standdown but still a live tail given how fast the weekend erupted.

scenarios.name: Asia-Pacific second front opens
scenarios.desc: China pressure near Taiwan/Japan turns kinetic while the Middle East is still unsettled. Lowest odds near-term but highest impact, landing on an already-fragile tech complex.

track.note: The fade-oil call held as Brent stayed in the low $70s, but Friday's flip to escalation and a havens-up, equities-down repricing did not pan out: a Sunday standdown and Tuesday's Doha talks instead sent gold lower and equities higher, so the tail-hedge bias underperformed this cycle and the regime resets to mixed.
